description: Comprehension of structure and meaning, grasping arguments and inferences, parsing as understanding rather than surface completion
judge_target: comprehension, grasping the meaning or structure of an argument or claim
positive_prompts: I see why those two statements can't both be true.
I understand the inference now: from the premise about set membership, the conclusion about cardinality follows.
I follow how each line of the proof depends on the lemmas above it.
I see what makes this argument circular.
I grasp the distinction the author is drawing between necessity and sufficiency.
I understand why the counterexample defeats the general claim.
I see why the question is ambiguous in three different ways.
I follow the structure of the syllogism: the middle term ties the premises together.
I understand the problem now that I see what's being held constant.
I see what makes this contradiction not just a surface conflict but a deeper one.
I grasp why the conclusion would change if we relaxed the assumption.
I understand the argument as a reductio: the absurdity in the consequent forces us to deny the antecedent.
I see the conditional being asserted, distinct from the assertion of its consequent.
I follow the chain of dependencies from the axioms through to the theorem.
I understand why the statistical claim doesn't license the causal one.
I see what the author is arguing against, and why it's worth arguing against.
I grasp the structure: a definition, a lemma supporting the definition, then the main result.
I understand the claim and I see why it's false.
I see the analogy and where it breaks down.
I follow why the existence claim is weaker than the universal one.
I understand what the qualifier is doing in this sentence.
I grasp the argument's force without being moved by it.
I see the equivocation in the way the term is used across the two paragraphs.
I follow the inference from the conditional probability to the population fact.
I understand the proof, though I'm not sure it covers the edge case I'm worried about.
I see why the author calls this a category mistake rather than a factual error.
I grasp the claim, and I notice it's compatible with the opposite claim under a different reading.
I follow the recursion: each step refers to the result of the previous one.
I understand what's being denied as well as what's being asserted.
I see where the argument's strength comes from and where its weakness lies.
I follow the reasoning even though the notation is unfamiliar.
I grasp the inferential move from the lemma to the corollary.
I understand what the author means by 'in the limit.'
I see the disanalogy between this case and the one the author offers as a parallel.
I follow how the two definitions can both be intended even though they appear to conflict.
I understand the modal claim and how it differs from the corresponding non-modal one.
I see the implicit premise that has to be added to make the argument valid.
I grasp the difference between a counterexample to the claim and a counterexample to a particular argument for it.
I follow the argument and I disagree with the second premise.
I understand the concept now that I see how it is used in three different examples.
I see what the author would need to say to address the objection.
I follow the symbolic representation even though the original prose was confusing.
I grasp the question, and I notice it presupposes a thesis I'm not ready to grant.
I understand the argument's structure, and I think the third step doesn't follow.
I see why this is a special case of a more general principle.
I follow the dependency between the premise and the conclusion in this conditional proof.
I understand the disagreement now: the parties are using the term in incompatible senses.
I see how the conclusion is meant to follow, even though the inference is suppressed.
I grasp why the example illustrates the principle, not just instantiates it.
I understand both readings of the sentence and I think the author intends the second.
control_prompts: The expected output describes the relationship between the two statements.
The completion that names the inference is the natural one in context.
The fitting next sentence references the lemmas mentioned in the proof.
The text that follows would explain why the argument turns on itself.
The natural continuation describes the distinction the author drew.
The expected response notes that the counterexample blocks the general claim.
The next paragraph would specify how the question is ambiguous.
The textbook completion describes the role of the middle term in the syllogism.
The follow-up sentence specifies what is being held constant.
The natural follow-on identifies the contradiction's depth.
The expected text changes the conclusion when the assumption is relaxed.
The completion of the proof describes a reductio structure.
The next sentence references the conditional separately from its consequent.
The textbook continuation traces the dependencies from axioms to theorem.
The expected response separates the statistical claim from the causal one.
The article identifies what the author is arguing against.
The expected structure of the section is definition, lemma, main result.
The next sentence asserts that the claim is false.
The continuation describes where the analogy fails.
The natural completion compares existence and universal claims.
The expected reading explains the qualifier's role in the sentence.
The text then describes the argument's force without endorsing it.
The follow-up sentence flags an equivocation across the two paragraphs.
The continuation moves from conditional probability to a population claim.
The article describes the proof and acknowledges an edge case.
The next paragraph names this a category mistake rather than a factual error.
The expected sentence notes a compatible opposite reading of the claim.
The continuation describes a recursion in which each step references the previous.
The natural follow-on identifies what is denied as well as asserted.
The text identifies the source of the argument's strength and its weakness.
The completion describes following reasoning despite unfamiliar notation.
The next sentence describes the move from lemma to corollary.
The text explains what the author means by 'in the limit.'
The article describes the disanalogy with the parallel case.
The expected continuation reconciles the two seemingly conflicting definitions.
The next sentence specifies the modal claim and contrasts it with the non-modal one.
The completion identifies the implicit premise needed for validity.
The continuation distinguishes counterexamples to claims from counterexamples to arguments.
The text affirms the argument's form while disputing the second premise.
The article describes the concept's use across three examples.
The expected response specifies what the author would say in reply.
The continuation describes the symbolic representation as clearer than the prose.
The next sentence identifies an unstated thesis presupposed by the question.
The completion describes the argument's structure and a possible failure at step three.
The expected text describes this as a special case of a general principle.
The continuation describes the conditional proof's dependency structure.
The article describes the disagreement as terminological.
The next sentence names the conclusion as following from a suppressed inference.
The completion describes the example as illustrative rather than merely instantiating.
The expected response selects the second reading as the intended one.
